#3.1.2. Import the dataset medical_data.csv.

hypertension <- read_csv("medical_data.csv")

IR

#3.1.6b)visualize class imbalance ratio in percentage

hypertension %>%
  count(CVD) %>%
  mutate(Percent = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = CVD, y = n, fill = CVD)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(Percent,1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "CVD Class Distribution with Percentages",
       x = "CVD",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

3.2 Task 2: Data Preprocessing (15 Marks) #3.2.1. Handle missing values (justify your method: removal or imputation).

hypertension_clean <- na.omit(hypertension)

Missing values were handled using removal because the dataset contained no significant missing data,this is to ensure data completeness without introducing bias from imputation.

#3.2.2. Convert categorical variables to factors:

categorical_vars <- c("gender", "smoking", "alcohol",
                      "family_history", "medication_adherence", "urban_residence")

hypertension_clean[categorical_vars] <- lapply(hypertension_clean[categorical_vars], as.factor)

#3.2.3a) Convert the target variable CVD to a factor with levels “No” (0) and “Yes” (1).

hypertension_clean$CVD <- factor(hypertension_clean$CVD,
                                 levels = c("No", "Yes"))

#3.2.5b.outlier visualization

boxplot(hypertension_clean$age, main="Outliers_age")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$gender, main="Outliers_gender")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$bmi, main="Outliers_bmi")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$blood_pressure, main="Outliers_blood_pressure")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$cholesterol, main="Outliers_cholesterol")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$glucose, main="Outliers_glucose")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$smoking, main="Outliers_smoking")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$alcohol, main="Outliers_alcohol")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$physical_activity, main="Outliers_physical_activity")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$family_history, main="Outliers_family_history")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$stress_level, main="Outliers_stress_level")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$heart_rate, main="Outliers_heart_rate")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$sleep_hours, main="Outliers_sleep_hours")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$medication_adherence, main="Outliers_medication_adherence")

boxplot(hypertension_clean$urban_residence, main="Outliers_urban_residence")

Function to remove outliers using IQR from a single column

remove_outliers_iqr <- function(x) {
  Q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  Q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  IQR <- Q3 - Q1
  lower_bound <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
  upper_bound <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
  return(x >= lower_bound & x <= upper_bound)  # Returns TRUE for non-outliers
}

# Apply to all numeric columns in your dataset
numeric_cols <- names(hypertension_clean)[sapply(hypertension_clean, is.numeric)]

# Create logical vector for rows to KEEP (no outliers in ANY column)
keep_rows <- rep(TRUE, nrow(hypertension_clean))

for(col in numeric_cols) {
  keep_rows <- keep_rows & remove_outliers_iqr(hypertension_clean[[col]])
}

# Remove rows with outliers
hypertension_cleant <- hypertension_clean[keep_rows, ]

cat("Original rows:", nrow(hypertension_clean))
## Original rows: 2500
cat("Rows after outlier removal:", nrow(hypertension_cleant))
## Rows after outlier removal: 2355
cat("Rows removed:", nrow(hypertension_clean) - nrow(hypertension_cleant))
## Rows removed: 145

N/B : hypertension_cleant DOES NOT HAVE OUTLIERS

#3.2.3b)verify factors conversion

str(hypertension_cleant)
## tibble [2,355 × 16] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ age                 : num [1:2355] 36.6 41.5 68.4 46.1 46.9 ...
##  $ gender              : Factor w/ 2 levels "0","1": 1 2 2 2 1 1 2 2 1 1 ...
##  $ bmi                 : num [1:2355] 19.9 32.1 30.4 25.5 26.9 ...
##  $ blood_pressure      : num [1:2355] 127 126 105 140 108 ...

##  $ glucose             : num [1:2355] 105.8 76.9 101.6 130.7 58.7 ...

##  $ alcohol             : Factor w/ 2 levels "0","1": 1 2 1 1 1 1 1 2 1 2 ...
##  $ physical_activity   : num [1:2355] 1.66 1.55 3.9 3.49 1.58 ...
##  $ family_history      : Factor w/ 2 levels "0","1": 1 1 1 2 1 1 2 1 1 1 ...
##  $ stress_level        : num [1:2355] 3.327 4.559 0.793 1.664 2.804 ...
##  $ heart_rate          : num [1:2355] 77.8 79.9 77.9 83.5 90.3 ...
##  $ sleep_hours         : num [1:2355] 5.81 5.3 7.87 7.78 7.31 ...
##  $ medication_adherence: Factor w/ 2 levels "0","1": 2 1 2 2 1 2 2 2 2 1 ...

#3.2.4. For KNN and SVM:Scale numeric predictors(mean=0, sd=1)using preProcess()from caret. #3.2.4a) site numerical variables

numeric_vars <- names(hypertension_cleant)[sapply(hypertension_cleant, is.numeric)]

numeric_vars
## [1] "age"               "bmi"               "blood_pressure"   
## [4] "cholesterol"       "glucose"           "physical_activity"
## [7] "stress_level"      "heart_rate"        "sleep_hours"

#3.2.4b)Scale numeric predictors

 numeric_vars <- c("age", "bmi", "blood_pressure", "cholesterol",
                  "glucose", "stress_level", "heart_rate", "sleep_hours")

preproc <- preProcess(hypertension_cleant[, numeric_vars],
                      method = c("center", "scale"))

hypertension_scaled <- hypertension_cleant

hypertension_scaled[, numeric_vars] <- predict(preproc,
                                               hypertension_cleant[, numeric_vars])

#3.2.4c)verify scaling using numerical variable ‘age’

cat("\n========== SCALING VERIFICATION ==========\n")
## 
## ========== SCALING VERIFICATION ==========
cat("Original age - Mean:", mean(hypertension_cleant$age), "| SD:", sd(hypertension_clean$age), "\n")
## Original age - Mean: 45.03592 | SD: 14.83276
cat("Scaled age - Mean:", round(mean(hypertension_scaled$age), 2), "| SD:", round(sd(hypertension_scaled$age), 2), "\n")
## Scaled age - Mean: 0 | SD: 1

#3.2.5a. For Random Forest: Scaling is not required (tree-based). Scaling is not required for Random Forest because it is a tree-based algorithm that splits data using thresholds rather than distance or magnitude. Therefore, changes in scale do not affect model performance or splits.

#3.2.6. Document your preprocessing decisions in the report. handling missing values’ scalling conversion to factors removal of outliers

3.3 Task 3: Data Partitioning (10 Marks)

#3.3.1. Set the seed to 2026 for reproducibility.

 set.seed(2026)

for the purpose of fare comparison i will partition scaled data and my train data will have no outliers and will also be scaled and i will use this acreoss all the 4 models

#3.3.2. Split the data into 70% training and 30% testing using stratified sampling (to preserve class balance).

 train_index <- createDataPartition(hypertension_scaled$CVD, p = 0.70,
list = FALSE)
train_data <- hypertension_cleant[train_index, ]
test_data <- hypertension_cleant[-train_index, ]

#train for svm and knn scaled
train_scaled <- hypertension_scaled[train_index, ]
test_scaled <- hypertension_scaled[-train_index, ]


dim(train_data)
## [1] 1650   16
dim(test_data)
## [1] 705  16
cat("\n========================\n")
## 
## ========================
dim(train_scaled)
## [1] 1650   16
dim(test_scaled)
## [1] 705  16

3.4 Task 4: TrainControl Scheme (10 Marks)

#3.4.1. Define a trainControl scheme with the following specifications:

ctrl <- trainControl(
method = "cv",              # 10-fold cross-validation
number = 10,                # 10 folds
savePredictions = "final",  # Save predictions
classProbs = TRUE,          # Enable probability predictions
verboseIter = FALSE,        # ROC, Sens, Spec

summaryFunction = twoClassSummary, # Suppress iteration output
)

#3.4.2. Explain why cross-validation is important for model evaluation. Reduces overfitting by evaluating model on multiple unseen folds Provides more stable and reliable performance estimates than a single train-test split Allows hyperparameter tuning without leaking test data Maximizes use of limited training data

#3.4.3. Save this control object to use across all models. For fair comparison

3.5 Task 5: Model Training- Logistic Regression (15 Marks)

#3.5.1. Train a Logistic Regression model using caret::train() with method “glm”.

log_model <- train(
  
CVD ~ age + gender + bmi + blood_pressure + cholesterol + 
      glucose + smoking + alcohol + physical_activity + 
      family_history + stress_level + sleep_hours,

data = train_data,
method = "glm",
family = binomial,
trControl = ctrl
)
## Warning in train.default(x, y, weights = w, ...): The metric "Accuracy" was not
## in the result set. ROC will be used instead.

#3.5.3. Extract and report the model coefficients.

summary(log_model$finalModel)$coefficients
##                       Estimate  Std. Error   z value     Pr(>|z|)
## (Intercept)       -14.34677716 1.509714031 -9.502977 2.039747e-21
## age                 0.03862052 0.007034981  5.489782 4.024294e-08
## gender1             0.24532917 0.193834927  1.265660 2.056347e-01
## bmi                 0.10766928 0.021398824  5.031551 4.865272e-07
## blood_pressure      0.03197387 0.006916625  4.622756 3.786744e-06
## cholesterol         0.01380783 0.002582562  5.346564 8.963941e-08
## glucose             0.02658279 0.003601163  7.381724 1.562526e-13
## smoking1            0.78778524 0.205658776  3.830545 1.278596e-04
## alcohol1            0.22976114 0.196474841  1.169418 2.422354e-01
## physical_activity  -0.57030851 0.077907271 -7.320350 2.473245e-13
## family_history1     1.14139718 0.205422470  5.556340 2.754893e-08
## stress_level        0.29166205 0.050719721  5.750466 8.899756e-09
## sleep_hours        -0.53118614 0.074853963 -7.096299 1.281416e-12

#3.5.4a) Calculate Odds Ratios (OR) for at least 3 significant predictors: OR = eβ

# Calculate coefficients and odds ratios
coefficients <- coef(log_model$finalModel)
odds_ratios <- exp(coefficients)

# Create dataframe and sort
odds_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(odds_ratios),
  Odds_Ratio = round(odds_ratios, 3)
)

# Sort by Odds Ratio descending (largest to smallest)
odds_df <- odds_df[order(-odds_df$Odds_Ratio), ]

print(odds_df)
##                            Variable Odds_Ratio
## family_history1     family_history1      3.131
## smoking1                   smoking1      2.199
## stress_level           stress_level      1.339
## gender1                     gender1      1.278
## alcohol1                   alcohol1      1.258
## bmi                             bmi      1.114
## age                             age      1.039
## blood_pressure       blood_pressure      1.032
## glucose                     glucose      1.027
## cholesterol             cholesterol      1.014
## sleep_hours             sleep_hours      0.588
## physical_activity physical_activity      0.565
## (Intercept)             (Intercept)      0.000

#3.5.4b)odds table

odds_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(odds_ratios),
  Coefficient = round(coefficients, 4),
  Odds_Ratio = round(odds_ratios, 3)
)

odds_table <- odds_table[order(-abs(odds_table$Coefficient)), ]

odds_table
##                            Variable Coefficient Odds_Ratio
## (Intercept)             (Intercept)    -14.3468      0.000
## family_history1     family_history1      1.1414      3.131
## smoking1                   smoking1      0.7878      2.199
## physical_activity physical_activity     -0.5703      0.565
## sleep_hours             sleep_hours     -0.5312      0.588
## stress_level           stress_level      0.2917      1.339
## gender1                     gender1      0.2453      1.278
## alcohol1                   alcohol1      0.2298      1.258
## bmi                             bmi      0.1077      1.114
## age                             age      0.0386      1.039
## blood_pressure       blood_pressure      0.0320      1.032
## glucose                     glucose      0.0266      1.027
## cholesterol             cholesterol      0.0138      1.014

#3.5.4b)Bar plot of Odds Ratios

# Remove intercept for better visualization
odds_table <- odds_table[odds_table$Variable != "(Intercept)", ]

# Sort by Coefficient absolute value
odds_table <- odds_table[order(-abs(odds_table$Coefficient)), ]


ggplot(odds_table, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Odds_Ratio), y = Odds_Ratio, 
                       fill = Odds_Ratio > 1)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 1)+
  labs(title = "Odds Ratios for CVD Predictors",
       subtitle = "Values > 1 indicate risk factors, < 1 indicate protective factors",
       x = "Predictor Variable",
       y = "Odds Ratio") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "coral", "FALSE" = "steelblue"),
                    labels = c("Risk Factor", "Protective Factor")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Odds_Ratio, 2)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3)

#3.5.5. Interpret the Odds Ratios in the context of CVD risk. SLIDE

#3.5.2a) Alternatively, fit a baseline model using base R glm() function.

log_modelb <- glm(
  CVD ~ age + gender + bmi + blood_pressure + cholesterol + 
      glucose + smoking + alcohol + physical_activity + 
      family_history + stress_level + sleep_hours,
  
  data = train_data,
  family = binomial
)

summary(log_modelb)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = CVD ~ age + gender + bmi + blood_pressure + cholesterol + 
##     glucose + smoking + alcohol + physical_activity + family_history + 
##     stress_level + sleep_hours, family = binomial, data = train_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)       -14.346777   1.509714  -9.503  < 2e-16 ***
## age                 0.038621   0.007035   5.490 4.02e-08 ***
## gender1             0.245329   0.193835   1.266 0.205635    
## bmi                 0.107669   0.021399   5.032 4.87e-07 ***
## blood_pressure      0.031974   0.006917   4.623 3.79e-06 ***
## cholesterol         0.013808   0.002583   5.347 8.96e-08 ***
## glucose             0.026583   0.003601   7.382 1.56e-13 ***
## smoking1            0.787785   0.205659   3.831 0.000128 ***
## alcohol1            0.229761   0.196475   1.169 0.242235    
## physical_activity  -0.570309   0.077907  -7.320 2.47e-13 ***
## family_history1     1.141397   0.205422   5.556 2.75e-08 ***
## stress_level        0.291662   0.050720   5.750 8.90e-09 ***
## sleep_hours        -0.531186   0.074854  -7.096 1.28e-12 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 1068.37  on 1649  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance:  736.51  on 1637  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 762.51
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

#3.5.2b) Calculate Odds Ratios (OR) for at least 3 significant predictors: OR = eβ

# Extract coefficients
coef_vals <- coef(log_modelb)

# Convert to odds ratios
odds_ratios <- exp(coef_vals)

# Get p-values from model summary
p_values <- summary(log_modelb)$coefficients[, 4]

# Create clean table
odds_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(coef_vals),
  Coefficient = round(coef_vals, 4),
  Odds_Ratio = round(odds_ratios, 3),
  P_value = round(p_values, 4)
)

# Remove row names
rownames(odds_table) <- NULL

# Sort by importance 
odds_table <- odds_table[order(-abs(odds_table$Coefficient)), ]

odds_table
##             Variable Coefficient Odds_Ratio P_value
## 1        (Intercept)    -14.3468      0.000  0.0000
## 11   family_history1      1.1414      3.131  0.0000
## 8           smoking1      0.7878      2.199  0.0001
## 10 physical_activity     -0.5703      0.565  0.0000
## 13       sleep_hours     -0.5312      0.588  0.0000
## 12      stress_level      0.2917      1.339  0.0000
## 3            gender1      0.2453      1.278  0.2056
## 9           alcohol1      0.2298      1.258  0.2422
## 4                bmi      0.1077      1.114  0.0000
## 2                age      0.0386      1.039  0.0000
## 5     blood_pressure      0.0320      1.032  0.0000
## 7            glucose      0.0266      1.027  0.0000
## 6        cholesterol      0.0138      1.014  0.0000

3.6 Task 6: Model Training- KNN (15 Marks)

#3.6.1. Train a K-Nearest Neighbors model using caret::train() with method “knn”. #3.6.2a. Tune the parameter k (number of neighbors) using the grid: k = 1, 3, 5, …,21.

knn_grid <- data.frame(k = seq(1, 21, by = 2))

ctrl <- trainControl(
  method = "cv",
  number = 10,
  classProbs = TRUE,
  summaryFunction = twoClassSummary
)

knn_model <- train(
  CVD ~ age + gender + bmi + blood_pressure + cholesterol + 
      glucose + smoking + alcohol + physical_activity + 
      family_history + stress_level + sleep_hours,
  
  data = train_scaled,
  method = "knn",
  tuneGrid = knn_grid,
  trControl = ctrl,
  metric = "ROC"
)

knn_model$results
##     k       ROC      Sens       Spec      ROCSD      SensSD     SpecSD
## 1   1 0.5811268 0.9313713 0.23088235 0.04234468 0.017230050 0.08076715
## 2   3 0.7024859 0.9724061 0.14044118 0.07334404 0.008057955 0.06623998
## 3   5 0.7442492 0.9818293 0.07904412 0.06142558 0.008981603 0.07036171
## 4   7 0.7653270 0.9885589 0.06139706 0.05618521 0.007795499 0.08275553
## 5   9 0.7738476 0.9932705 0.05514706 0.05235339 0.007093644 0.06796777
## 6  11 0.7892383 0.9959641 0.04963235 0.05863651 0.005676335 0.04921072
## 7  13 0.7929041 0.9966443 0.04926471 0.05870218 0.004745683 0.06363587
## 8  15 0.7918003 0.9966443 0.01875000 0.04953034 0.007249151 0.04218428
## 9  17 0.7932266 0.9986577 0.03125000 0.05180190 0.004244668 0.05311479
## 10 19 0.8030002 0.9986577 0.01875000 0.03987453 0.004244668 0.03019037
## 11 21 0.8081906 0.9993289 0.01250000 0.03579093 0.002122334 0.03952847

#3.6.2a.plot KNN model

plot(knn_model)

#3.6.3. Important: Use the scaled dataset for KNN (distance-based algorithm).

#3.6.4. Report the optimal k value selected by cross-validation.

knn_model$bestTune
##     k
## 11 21

#3.6.5. Plot accuracy vs. k to visualize the tuning results.

ggplot(knn_model$results, aes(x = k, y = ROC)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "KNN Model Tuning (ROC vs k)",
       x = "Number of Neighbors (k)",
       y = "ROC AUC") +
  theme_minimal()

3.7 Task 7: Model Training- SVM (15 Marks)

#3.7.1. Train a Support Vector Machine model using caret::train() with method “svmRadial”. #3.7.2. Tune the hyperparameters:C (cost): 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, sigma: 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 #3.7.3. Important: Use the scaled dataset for SVM.

svm_model <- train(
  CVD ~ age + gender + bmi + blood_pressure + cholesterol + 
      glucose + smoking + alcohol + physical_activity + 
      family_history + stress_level + sleep_hours,

data = train_scaled, # Use scaled data!
method = "svmRadial",
trControl = ctrl,
tuneGrid = expand.grid(
C = c(0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4),
sigma = c(0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5)
))
## Warning in train.default(x, y, weights = w, ...): The metric "Accuracy" was not
## in the result set. ROC will be used instead.
svm_model$results
##       C sigma       ROC      Sens       Spec      ROCSD      SensSD     SpecSD
## 1  0.25  0.01 0.8253629 0.9973109 0.07352941 0.03320956 0.003471636 0.07647294
## 5  0.50  0.01 0.8254597 0.9966352 0.07389706 0.03079493 0.004752842 0.06404168
## 9  1.00  0.01 0.8248932 0.9939371 0.11544118 0.03200413 0.005902441 0.12982332

## 11 1.00  0.10 0.8145046 0.9892345 0.16397059 0.05893450 0.008499004 0.09986354
## 15 2.00  0.10 0.8159303 0.9905859 0.14007353 0.05831198 0.008490988 0.06368895
## 19 4.00  0.10 0.8104040 0.9919236 0.11507353 0.05645679 0.006183907 0.10591880

## 12 1.00  0.50 0.7410201 0.9872393 0.05477941 0.08316670 0.015321178 0.07269647
## 16 2.00  0.50 0.7387629 0.9892572 0.03639706 0.08177574 0.010586156 0.05119118
## 20 4.00  0.50 0.7342192 0.9879104 0.03014706 0.08000878 0.012963516 0.04219674

#3.7.4a. Report the optimal C and sigma values.

 svm_model$bestTune
##   sigma   C
## 5  0.01 0.5

#3.7.5. Explain the role of the kernel function in SVM.

#3.7.4b.Plot Heatmap of C vs Sigma for SVM

# Extract SVM tuning results
svm_results <- svm_model$results

# Create heatmap
ggplot(svm_results, aes(x = factor(C), y = factor(sigma), fill = ROC)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(ROC, 3)), size = 4, color = "black") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightyellow", high = "darkred", 
                      name = "ROC AUC",
                      limits = c(min(svm_results$ROC), max(svm_results$ROC))) +
  labs(title = "SVM Hyperparameter Tuning Heatmap",
       subtitle = "ROC AUC by Cost (C) and Sigma (γ)",
       x = "Cost Parameter (C)",
       y = "Sigma (γ) - Kernel Width") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
        legend.position = "right")

# Save the plot
ggsave("svm_heatmap.png", width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)

3.8 Task 8: Model Training- Random Forest (15 Marks)

#3.8.1. Train a Random Forest model using caret::train() with method “rf”. #3.8.2. Tune the parameter mtry (features per split): 2, 4, 6, 8. #3.8.3. Set ntree = 500 (number of trees).

 rf_model <- train(
   CVD ~ age + gender + bmi + blood_pressure + cholesterol + 
      glucose + smoking + alcohol + physical_activity + 
      family_history + stress_level + sleep_hours,
  
  data = train_data, # Original data (not scaled)
method = "rf",
trControl = ctrl,
tuneGrid = expand.grid(mtry = c(2, 4, 6, 8)),
ntree = 500
)
## Warning in train.default(x, y, weights = w, ...): The metric "Accuracy" was not
## in the result set. ROC will be used instead.
rf_model$results 
##   mtry       ROC      Sens       Spec      ROCSD      SensSD     SpecSD
## 1    2 0.8448032 0.9993243 0.00000000 0.05635646 0.002136674 0.00000000
## 2    4 0.8345236 0.9979820 0.02463235 0.06062155 0.003249244 0.03181794
## 3    6 0.8276927 0.9966352 0.05551471 0.05953282 0.004767098 0.04603180
## 4    8 0.8236233 0.9952884 0.08602941 0.06798036 0.005543124 0.06026621

#3.8.4. Report the optimal mtry value.

rf_model$bestTune
##   mtry
## 1    2

#3.8.5. Extract and plot variable importance.

varImp(rf_model)
## rf variable importance
## 
##                    Overall
## physical_activity 100.0000
## glucose            98.8704
## age                95.9871
## sleep_hours        89.9894
## stress_level       83.2361
## cholesterol        80.6201
## bmi                80.1646
## blood_pressure     78.5956
## family_history1     8.6474
## smoking1            2.2121
## alcohol1            0.2222
## gender1             0.0000
plot(varImp(rf_model), main = "Random Forest: Variable
Importance")

#3.8.6a. Identify the top 5 most important predictors for CVD.

rf_imp <- varImp(rf_model)

rf_imp_df <- rf_imp$importance
rf_imp_df$Variable <- rownames(rf_imp_df)

#3.8.6b.top 5 predictors

top5 <- rf_imp_df[order(-rf_imp_df$Overall), ][1:5, ]
top5
##                     Overall          Variable
## physical_activity 100.00000 physical_activity
## glucose            98.87037           glucose
## age                95.98712               age
## sleep_hours        89.98943       sleep_hours
## stress_level       83.23608      stress_level

#3.8.6b.plot Top 5 Important Predictors for CVD

ggplot(top5, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 5 Important Predictors for CVD",
       x = "Predictor",
       y = "Importance Score") +
  theme_minimal()

3.9 Task 9: Model Evaluation and Comparison (20 Marks)

#3.9.1. Predict the class and probabilities on the test set for all 4 models.

log_pred <- predict(log_model, test_data)
log_probs <- predict(log_model, test_data, type = "prob")

knn_pred <- predict(knn_model, test_scaled)
knn_probs <- predict(knn_model, test_scaled, type = "prob")

svm_pred <- predict(svm_model, test_scaled)
svm_probs <- predict(svm_model, test_scaled, type = "prob")

rf_pred <- predict(rf_model, test_data)
rf_probs <- predict(rf_model, test_data, type = "prob")

#3.9.2a. For each model, generate a Confusion Matrix and report: •Accuracy,•Sensitivity (Recall),•Specificity,•Precision (PPV),•F1-Score,•Kappa #3.9.2a: Confusion Matrix and report (logistic regression )

confusionMatrix(log_pred, test_data$CVD, positive = "Yes")

## 
##           Reference
## Prediction  No Yes
##        No  623  49
##        Yes  13  20

##                  95% CI : (0.8887, 0.9319)

##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.2065

##    Detection Prevalence : 0.04681

##        'Positive' Class : Yes             
##

confusionMatrix(svm_pred, test_scaled$CVD, positive = "Yes")

confusionMatrix(rf_pred, test_data$CVD, positive = "Yes")
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction  No Yes

##        Yes   4   2

##

#3.9.3. Plot ROC curves for all 4 models on the same graph. #3.9.3a) get roc

log_roc <- roc(test_data$CVD, log_probs$Yes)
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
knn_roc <- roc(test_data$CVD, knn_probs$Yes)
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
svm_roc <- roc(test_data$CVD, svm_probs$Yes)
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
rf_roc <- roc(test_data$CVD, rf_probs$Yes)
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases

#3.9.3b)Plot ROC curves for all 4 models on the same graph.

plot(log_roc, col = "blue", lwd = 2, main = "ROC Comparison")
 lines(knn_roc, col = "green", lwd = 2)
  lines(svm_roc, col = "red", lwd = 2)
  lines(rf_roc, col = "purple", lwd = 2)
  legend("bottomright",
         legend = c("Logistic", "KNN", "SVM", "RF"),
col = c("blue", "green", "red", "purple"),
lwd = 2)

#3.9.4. Calculate and compare AUC values for all models.

auc_values <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Logistic", "KNN", "SVM", "Random Forest"),
AUC = c(as.numeric(auc(log_roc)),
as.numeric(auc(knn_roc)),
as.numeric(auc(svm_roc)),
as.numeric(auc(rf_roc)))
)
print(auc_values)
##           Model       AUC
## 1      Logistic 0.8460942
## 2           KNN 0.7483821
## 3           SVM 0.8229879
## 4 Random Forest 0.7838050

#3.9.5a) Create a comparison table summarizing all metrics.

model_performance <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Logistic Regression", "KNN", "SVM", "Random Forest"),
  auc_values
)
model_performance <- model_performance[order(-model_performance$AUC), ]
model_performance
##                 Model       Model.1       AUC
## 1 Logistic Regression      Logistic 0.8460942
## 3                 SVM           SVM 0.8229879
## 4       Random Forest Random Forest 0.7838050
## 2                 KNN           KNN 0.7483821

#3.9.6. Rank the models from best to worst based on AUC

ggplot(model_performance, aes(x = reorder(Model, AUC), y = AUC)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Model Comparison Based on AUC",
       x = "Model",
       y = "AUC Score") +
  theme_minimal()

#3.9.5B) Create a comparison table summarizing all metrics.

# Plot all ROC curves
plot(log_roc, col = "blue", lwd = 2, main = "ROC Comparison")
lines(knn_roc, col = "green", lwd = 2)
lines(svm_roc, col = "red", lwd = 2)
lines(rf_roc, col = "purple", lwd = 2)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = c("Logistic", "KNN", "SVM", "RF"),
       col = c("blue", "green", "red", "purple"),
       lwd = 2)

# AUC Comparison
auc_values <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Logistic", "KNN", "SVM", "Random Forest"),
  AUC = c(as.numeric(auc(log_roc)),
          as.numeric(auc(knn_roc)),
          as.numeric(auc(svm_roc)),
          as.numeric(auc(rf_roc)))
)

#3.9.2. For each model, generate a Confusion Matrix and report:

# ============================================
# Function to extract all metrics from confusion matrix
# ============================================
extract_all_metrics <- function(cm, probs, actual, model_name) {
  # Calculate AUC
  roc_obj <- roc(actual, probs$Yes)
  auc_value <- as.numeric(auc(roc_obj))
  
  # Extract metrics from confusion matrix
  metrics <- data.frame(
    Model = model_name,
    Accuracy = round(cm$overall["Accuracy"], 4),
    Sensitivity = round(cm$byClass["Sensitivity"], 4),
    Specificity = round(cm$byClass["Specificity"], 4),
    Precision = round(cm$byClass["Precision"], 4),
    F1_Score = round(cm$byClass["F1"], 4),
    Kappa = round(cm$overall["Kappa"], 4),
    AUC = round(auc_value, 4)
  )
  
  return(metrics)
}

# ============================================
# Generate confusion matrices and extract metrics
# ============================================

# Logistic Regression
cm_log <- confusionMatrix(log_pred, test_data$CVD, positive = "Yes")
metrics_log <- extract_all_metrics(cm_log, log_probs, test_data$CVD, "Logistic Regression")
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
# KNN
cm_knn <- confusionMatrix(knn_pred, test_scaled$CVD, positive = "Yes")
metrics_knn <- extract_all_metrics(cm_knn, knn_probs, test_scaled$CVD, "KNN")
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
# SVM
cm_svm <- confusionMatrix(svm_pred, test_scaled$CVD, positive = "Yes")
metrics_svm <- extract_all_metrics(cm_svm, svm_probs, test_scaled$CVD, "SVM")
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
# Random Forest
cm_rf <- confusionMatrix(rf_pred, test_data$CVD, positive = "Yes")
metrics_rf <- extract_all_metrics(cm_rf, rf_probs, test_data$CVD, "Random Forest")
## Setting levels: control = No, case = Yes
## Setting direction: controls < cases
# ============================================
# Combine all metrics into one table
# ============================================
comparison_table <- rbind(metrics_log, metrics_knn, metrics_svm, metrics_rf)

# ============================================
# Display the table
# ============================================
print(comparison_table)
##                         Model Accuracy Sensitivity Specificity Precision
## Accuracy  Logistic Regression   0.9121      0.2899      0.9796    0.6061
## Accuracy1                 KNN   0.9021      0.0290      0.9969    0.5000
## Accuracy2                 SVM   0.9064      0.1014      0.9937    0.6364
## Accuracy3       Random Forest   0.8993      0.0290      0.9937    0.3333
##           F1_Score  Kappa    AUC
## Accuracy    0.3922 0.3511 0.8461
## Accuracy1   0.0548 0.0445 0.7484
## Accuracy2   0.1750 0.1522 0.8230
## Accuracy3   0.0533 0.0383 0.7838
# ============================================
# Save to CSV
# ============================================
write.csv(comparison_table, "model_comparison_metrics.csv", row.names = FALSE)

# ============================================
# Create a formatted table for presentation
# ============================================
library(knitr)
kable(comparison_table, 
      caption = "Model Performance Comparison - All Metrics",
      col.names = c("Model", "Accuracy", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", 
                    "Precision", "F1-Score", "Kappa", "AUC"),
      digits = 4)
Model Performance Comparison - All Metrics
Model Accuracy Sensitivity Specificity Precision F1-Score Kappa AUC
Accuracy Logistic Regression 0.9121 0.2899 0.9796 0.6061 0.3922 0.3511 0.8461
Accuracy1 KNN 0.9021 0.0290 0.9969 0.5000 0.0548 0.0445 0.7484
Accuracy2 SVM 0.9064 0.1014 0.9937 0.6364 0.1750 0.1522 0.8230
Accuracy3 Random Forest 0.8993 0.0290 0.9937 0.3333 0.0533 0.0383 0.7838

3.10 Task 10: Interpretation and Recommendations (15 Marks)

#3.10.1. Based on your results, answer the following: #3.10.1a) Which model performed best? Justify using metrics. Logistic regression. Highest AUC (0.85) Highest sensitivity (28.99%) Highest kappa (0.351) Best balanced accuracy (63.47%) Interpretable odds ratios for clinical insights

#3.10.1b)Which model performed worst? Explain possible reasons. Random forest 2nd lowest AUC (0.790) sensitivity (0%) kappa (0.351) Best balanced accuracy (63.47%) Interpretable odds ratios for clinical insights

#3.10.1c)Was feature scaling necessary for all models? Explain. feature scaling was nescessary for KNN,SVM Which are distance based model but not necessary for random forest a treebased model

#3.10.1d)Did cross-validation improve model reliability? Explain. Yes cross validation avoided overfitting, and uniformity among all the models ensuarig reliabilty.

#3.10.2. For the best model, identify the top 3 risk factors for CVD.

# Remove intercept
odds_table <- odds_table[odds_table$Variable != "(Intercept)", ]

# Sort by absolute coefficient (model importance)
odds_table <- odds_table[order(-abs(odds_table$Coefficient)), ]

# Take top 5
top5_coef <- head(odds_table, 5)

# Plot
ggplot(top5_coef, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Odds_Ratio), y = Odds_Ratio, 
                       fill = Odds_Ratio > 1)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Top 5 Most Important CVD Predictors",
       subtitle = "Based on Logistic Regression Coefficient Magnitude",
       x = "Predictor Variable",
       y = "Odds Ratio") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "coral", "FALSE" = "steelblue"),
                    labels = c("Risk Factor", "Protective Factor")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Odds_Ratio, 2)), hjust = -0.2, size = 4)

#3.10.3. Discuss the trade-offs between model accuracy and interpretability. Logistic regression: lower complexity, HIGH interpretability, GOOD accuracy • Random forest: higher accuracy, LOW interpretability (no direction) • SVM: moderate accuracy, VERY LOW interpretability (black box)

#3.10.4. Provide recommendations for healthcare practitioners based on your findings. (1) Based on this analysis, healthcare practitioners should prioritize lifestyle interventions for CVD prevention: (2) prescribing physical activity as medicine, (3) counseling patients on sleep hygiene (7-9 hours),get enough rest (4) implementing aggressive smoking cessation programs. (5) Patients with family history of CVD warrant earlier and more frequent screening.

#3.10.5. Discuss any limitations of your analysis (e.g., observational data, potential confounders). Observational data,Class imbalance ,Data limitations,Model limitations,Clinical caution,Outliers
